summary:
The Roman Kingdom (traditionally 753–509 BC) was the monarchical period of early Rome, during which the city was ruled by seven kings (Romulus, Numa Pompilius, Tullus Hostilius, Ancus Marcius, Tarquinius Priscus, Servius Tullius, and Tarquinius Superbus) whose reigns are recorded in a complex tradition mixing history, legend, and Etruscan-influenced lore. While the historicity of specific events and individual kings is debated, the period represents Rome's formative centuries during which its basic religious, military, and social institutions were established.

The later kings (Tarquinius Priscus and Tarquinius Superbus) were Etruscans or Etruscan-influenced, and their rule saw Rome develop into an urban center with Etruscan-style temples, drainage systems (the Cloaca Maxima), and urban planning. The expulsion of the last king Tarquinius Superbus ('the Proud') in 509 BC — triggered by the rape of the noblewoman Lucretia by his son Sextus — led to the establishment of the Republic. The kings' actual powers remain debated by historians; the Roman tradition may have retrospectively projected later Republican magistracies backward onto the monarchical period. The deep Roman hostility to kingship that persisted through the Republic (calling someone 'king' was a grave political insult) was rooted in this founding narrative.
